entry.title: Inquiry Regarding SAP Cloud ALM - Extending Transport Import Checks to Test Systems
entry.content: Based on my understanding, the Downgrade Protection (DGP) and Cross-Reference Checks in SAP Cloud ALM are currently limited to transports targeting the production system. We have a strong interest in extending this functionality to also cover transports destined for the test system. After reviewing the SAP Road Map Explorer, we could not find any announcements regarding this development. Therefore, I wonder if there are any plans to enable these checks for non-production environments in a future release?

entry.title: Re: Downstream Refining – SAP S/4HANA Capabilities for Multi‑Stage Cost & Value Formation and Tr
entry.content:
Dear SAP,

After reviewing in detail all the official documentation available, including OSS Notes, Help Portal, Best Practices, Transition Worklists, and the 2025 roadmap information, I have not found any official guidance covering the downstream valuation scenarios typical of refining, in particular:

- Multi‑stage production chains (multi‑stage refining chains)
- Blends, co‑products and yield‑shifting
- Circular flows and recirculations
- Market‑based absorption and valuation models (for example, methods such as isomargin, netback, target margin, parity pricing, etc.)
- Integration of external valuation models with Material Ledger
- Alignment between market‑based models, Actual Costing, IFRS and Margin Analysis

I have reviewed the relevant IS‑OIL OSS Notes (2328419, 3036174, 3183063, 308021, 3507981, 3008407). These notes cover the data model, HPM, UoM, LIS, migration and consistency, but none of them address downstream valuation or the cost models applied in refining.

Given that these scenarios represent the operational reality of downstream, where approximately 95% of the value comes from crude oil and only 5% from internal processing, I would like to understand SAP’s official position in order to remain aligned with SAP best practice.

Specifically, I would appreciate confirmation of the following:

1. Whether SAP provides an official reference model for downstream valuation (Industry Reference Architecture, Solution Maps, internal guides, etc.).
2. Whether there is any technical or functional documentation specific to refining (whitepapers, architecture guides, industry best practices).
3. Which parts of the model should be implemented using standard functionality and which parts require extensions, particularly regarding:
- integration of external valuation models with Material Ledger
- handling of blends and co‑products
- multi‑stage valuation
- circular flows
- market‑based valuation (including methods such as isomargin, netback, target margin, parity pricing, etc.)
- interaction with Margin Analysis and IFRS

In SAP Signavio Process Navigator, I noticed that in the Business Process Variant “Financial Planning & Analysis (Oil & Gas)”, the Business Capability “Inventory Accounting” appears under “Perform Management Accounting”, with a generic description and an explicit reference to “oil and gas upstream”.

However, according to the SAP Solution Roadmap for Oil, Gas & Energy (2025) and the functional documentation of S/4HANA Oil & Gas, Inventory Accounting is a Downstream capability, especially relevant for:

- Refining
- Hydrocarbon Supply Chain
- Terminal Operations
- Wet Stock Control
- Gain/Loss Accounting
- Quantity Conversion
- Tank Management

Likewise, a question arises regarding the specific solution processes for Downstream Inventory Accounting. In practice, these processes appear published under other functional groups, such as “PLO Primary Logistics Operations”, which does not align with the placement of the Business Capability “Inventory Accounting” within the “Financial Planning and Analysis (Oil & Gas)” variant.

Could SAP confirm whether the Solution Processes published in Signavio are aligned with the SAP Solution Roadmap for Oil, Gas & Energy (2025)?
And could you clarify whether the placement of “Inventory Accounting” within the FP&A (Oil & Gas) variant is intended to represent Downstream, or if it is a generic classification not specific to industry context?

This clarification would help ensure correct interpretation of Downstream financial processes.

In the following images:
1. Financial Planning and Analysis (Oil & Gas)
2. Model Company for Oil and Gas
3. Plan Inventory in Terminals (PLO‑08)
For example, where can we locate, for IS‑OIL Refining, the diagrams for the processes related to:
- Material Ledger Actual Costing
- Legal IFRS inventory valuation
- COPA (Margin Analysis)

Any guidance you can provide would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you in advance.

Kind regards,
Gustavo

entry.title: FULL OUTER JOIN in ABAP SQL?
entry.content:
FULL OUTER JOIN is still not permitted directly in ABAP 7.58 (ABAP Keyword Documentation: SELECT, FROM JOIN).

What I'd like to do:

    SELECT COALESCE( ekkn~ebeln, ekbe~ebeln ) AS ebeln,
           COALESCE( ekkn~ebelp, ekbe~ebelp ) AS ebelp,
           COALESCE( ekkn~zekkn, ekbe~zekkn ) AS zekkn,
           ekbe~bewtp,
           ekkn~loekz
      FROM ekbe
           FULL OUTER JOIN ekkn     " <=== FULL not valid in ABAP SQL
             ON  ekkn~ebeln = ekbe~ebeln
             AND ekkn~ebelp = ekbe~ebelp
             AND ekkn~zekkn = ekbe~zekkn
      WHERE ...
      INTO TABLE @DATA(itab).

What is the most simple workaround?

Thanks.

Sandra

entry.title: oModel.read is not working - OData V2
entry.content:
Hi All,

I am new on the SAP BTP/BAS world and I am trying to read a oData model v2 but it is not working.

return Controller.extend("XXX", {
    onInit: async function () { 
      try {
        await this._PickedMaterials(); 
      } catch (e) {
        console.error(e);
        MessageBox.error("Not able to select picket items.");
      }
    },

And here is my funcion _Picked Material

  _PickedMaterials: async function () {
debugger;
var oModel = this.getOwnerComponent().getModel("C_EWM_ODOOVPGQ_2_CDS");
oModel.read("/C_EWM_ODOOVPGQ_2", {
        urlParameters: { "$top": 20 },
        success: function (data) {
          console.log("SUCCESS DATA:", data);
          console.log("RESULTS:", data.results);
          debugger; 
        },
        error: function (err) {
          console.error("READ ERROR:", err);
          debugger; 
        }
      });

The code reaches the first debugger in the function but it does not enter the sucess or the error conditions. 


oModel is correct:

 and on debug mode shows like this:


Then it gets to oModel.read("/C_EWM_ODOOVPGQ_2", { It just jumps straight away

I have some console.logs in place like 

oModel.setUseBatch(false);

oModel.attachRequestSent((e) => {
        console.log("RequestSent:", e.getParameters()); // url, method, async, etc.
      });

C_EWM_ODOOVPGQ_2 is maintained in the backend and response is 200
I can also see the Live data for C_EWM_ODOOVPGQ_2_CDS in Service Center in BAS
OData was added using Service Manager
There is a API being used and working in this app

Any idea what is wrong here?

Thanks
Andréa

entry.title: Re: FULL OUTER JOIN in ABAP SQL?
entry.content:
The most simple workaround is via a CTE (WITH), UNION DISTINCT (UNION, DISTINCT) and two LEFT OUTER JOIN (see "Result set for outer joins"):

    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_full_outer_join,
             ebeln TYPE ebeln,
             ebelp TYPE ebelp,
             zekkn TYPE dzekkn,
             bewtp TYPE ekbe-bewtp,
             loekz TYPE ekkn-loekz,
           END OF ty_full_outer_join.
    TYPES tt_full_outer_join TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_full_outer_join WITH EMPTY KEY.

    DATA(itab) = VALUE tt_full_outer_join( ).
    WITH +union AS (
        SELECT ebeln, ebelp, zekkn
          FROM ekbe
        UNION DISTINCT
        SELECT ebeln, ebelp, zekkn
          FROM ekkn )
    SELECT +union~ebeln,
           +union~ebelp,
           +union~zekkn,
           ekbe~bewtp,
           ekkn~loekz
      FROM +union
           LEFT OUTER JOIN ekbe
             ON  ekbe~ebeln = +union~ebeln
             AND ekbe~ebelp = +union~ebelp
             AND ekbe~zekkn = +union~zekkn
           LEFT OUTER JOIN ekkn
             ON  ekkn~ebeln = +union~ebeln
             AND ekkn~ebelp = +union~ebelp
             AND ekkn~zekkn = +union~zekkn
      WHERE ...
      INTO TABLE @itab.

entry.title: Re: Customer overwritten during CO-PA derivation (SD to C...
entry.content: Yes it’s not a bug, but a result of how KEDR derivation rules are configured. The fix is to adjust the overwrite flag or the sequence of derivation steps so that the SD customer is not replaced unless you explicitly want it to be.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Building Prompt Pipelines for SAP Workflows (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 3 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  GENERATIVE AI HUB

SAP Generative AI Hub: Building Prompt Pipelines for SAP Workflows

Four multi-step prompt pipelines for real SAP workloads — exception log triage, HANA code review, document processing, and master data enrichment — using sequential chaining and chain-of-thought reasoning with the generative-ai-hub-sdk and LangChain.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: A single prompt call handles simple tasks. Complex SAP workloads — triage, code review, document processing, data enrichment — need multi-step pipelines where each prompt builds on the output of the previous one. This article covers two core pipeline patterns (sequential chaining and chain-of-thought reasoning) and four working SAP pipeline implementations using a mix of direct SDK calls and LangChain, with the generative-ai-hub-sdk handling authentication throughout.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Part 1 covered setup and configuration. Part 2 covered the Python SDK with five single-call SAP use cases. This part moves from single calls to pipelines — chaining prompts together so the output of one step becomes the input of the next.

The distinction matters in practice. A single prompt call works well for isolated tasks — summarize this log, classify this record. But real SAP workflows are multi-step: assess severity first, then summarize based on severity, then recommend action based on both. That requires a pipeline.

THE TWO CORE PIPELINE PATTERNS 

Pattern 1: Sequential Prompt Chaining

Sequential chaining passes the output of one prompt directly as input to the next. Each step is focused on a single task, and the chain builds context progressively. This produces more reliable results than asking one prompt to do everything — the model performs better when each step has a narrow, well-defined responsibility.

Step 1: Classify → Step 2: Analyze → Step 3: Recommend

The key design principle: each step should do one thing well. Resist the temptation to combine steps. A prompt that classifies AND summarizes AND recommends produces lower quality output than three focused prompts in sequence.

Pattern 2: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Reasoning

Chain-of-thought prompting instructs the model to reason through a problem explicitly before producing a final answer. For SAP workloads where the output needs to be traceable and justifiable — risk assessments, code review findings, data quality decisions — CoT produces outputs that practitioners can audit and defend.

Input context →	Reasoning steps (visible) → Conclusion → Action

CoT is particularly valuable in enterprise SAP contexts because it makes the model's reasoning transparent. When a pipeline flags a transport as high-risk or recommends a data fix, stakeholders can see exactly why — not just what the conclusion was.

CoT increases token consumption and response time compared to direct answer prompts. Use it for high-stakes decisions (risk assessment, code review, data quality) where traceability justifies the cost. For high-volume classification or extraction tasks, direct prompts from Part 2 are more appropriate.

PIPELINE 1 OF 4 

  ⬡  SAP Exception Log Triage: Classify → Summarize → Recommend 

Exception Log Triage Pipeline

This pipeline takes raw SAP exception log text and runs it through three focused steps: first classify severity, then produce a structured summary calibrated to that severity level, then generate recommended actions appropriate for the severity. Splitting these into three steps produces materially better output than asking one prompt to do all three.

Raw log input →	Classify severity → Structured summary → Recommended actions

 

# Pipeline 1: SAP Exception Log Triage
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
# Step 1: Classify severity
step1_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis expert.
    Classify this SAP exception log severity as one of:
    CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
    Return ONLY the severity label. Nothing else.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Classify severity:\n{log_input}')
]
severity = llm(step1_messages).content.strip()
# Step 2: Structured summary — informed by severity from Step 1
step2_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content=f'''You are an SAP Basis expert.
    This is a {severity} severity SAP exception.
    Provide a structured summary with:
    - Root cause (one sentence)
    - Affected component
    - Business impact
    Be concise. Severity context: {severity}.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Summarize this log:\n{log_input}')
]
summary = llm(step2_messages).content.strip()
# Step 3: Recommended actions — uses both severity and summary
step3_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis expert.
    Based on the severity and summary provided, give:
    1. Immediate action (within 1 hour)
    2. Root cause fix
    3. Prevention measure
    Be specific to SAP. No generic advice.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Severity: {severity}\nSummary: {summary}\nLog: {log_input}')
]
actions = llm(step3_messages).content.strip()
# Final structured output
print(f'SEVERITY: {severity}')
print(f'\nSUMMARY:\n{summary}')
print(f'\nRECOMMENDED ACTIONS:\n{actions}')

Each step passes its output forward as context. Step 2 knows the severity before writing the summary. Step 3 knows both severity and summary before recommending actions. This progressive context building is what makes pipeline output significantly more useful than a single-prompt approach.

  PIPELINE 2 OF 4 

  ⬡  HANA Code Review: Analyze → Explain → Suggest Fix 

HANA Code Review Pipeline

This pipeline applies chain-of-thought reasoning to HANA SQL code review. Rather than asking the model to jump directly to recommendations, CoT forces it to reason through the code structure first — producing more accurate findings and traceable justifications that developers can act on with confidence.

HANA code input → CoT: reason through code → Structured findings → Suggested fixes

 

# Pipeline 2: HANA Code Review with Chain-of-Thought
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
# GPT-4o for HANA code — strongest on SAP SQL specifics
 
hana_code = '''
  CREATE PROCEDURE LOAD_DELTA_ORDERS
  AS BEGIN
    DECLARE lv_count INT;
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO lv_count FROM SRC.ORDERS
    WHERE CHANGE_DATE > (SELECT MAX(LOAD_DATE) FROM CTRL_TABLE);
    INSERT INTO TGT.ORDERS_DELTA
    SELECT * FROM SRC.ORDERS
    WHERE CHANGE_DATE > (SELECT MAX(LOAD_DATE) FROM CTRL_TABLE);
    UPDATE CTRL_TABLE SET LOAD_DATE = CURRENT_DATE;
  END;
'''
# Step 1: CoT reasoning — model thinks before it concludes
step1_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL architect.
    Think through this HANA procedure step by step before forming conclusions.
    Reason about:
    - Data type risks and overflow potential
    - Performance implications (full scans, missing indexes)
    - Transaction safety and rollback handling
    - Subquery execution patterns
    Show your reasoning explicitly before any findings.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Review this HANA procedure:\n{hana_code}')
]
cot_reasoning = llm(step1_messages).content.strip()
# Step 2: Structured findings — informed by CoT reasoning
step2_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''Based on your reasoning, produce structured findings.
    Format each finding as:
    ISSUE: [description]
    SEVERITY: Critical / High / Medium / Low
    LINE: [line number or section]
    No additional commentary.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Reasoning:\n{cot_reasoning}\n\nCode:\n{hana_code}')
]
findings = llm(step2_messages).content.strip()
# Step 3: Suggested fixes for each finding
step3_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL developer.
    For each finding, provide the corrected HANA SQL code.
    Show original line and corrected replacement.
    Explain the fix in one sentence per issue.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Findings:\n{findings}\n\nOriginal code:\n{hana_code}')
]
fixes = llm(step3_messages).content.strip()
 
print(f'REASONING:\n{cot_reasoning}')
print(f'\nFINDINGS:\n{findings}')
print(f'\nSUGGESTED FIXES:\n{fixes}')

The CoT reasoning step is the key differentiator here. Without it, the model jumps to conclusions. With it, the findings section is grounded in explicit reasoning that a developer or architect can verify — which is critical for code review outputs that will be acted on in production systems.

PIPELINE 3 OF 4 

  ⬡  SAP Document Processing: Parse → Extract → Structure 

Document Processing Pipeline

SAP landscapes accumulate large volumes of semi-structured documents — functional specs, migration runbooks, transport logs, configuration workbooks. This pipeline uses LangChain's prompt templates to process documents consistently at scale: parse the document type and structure, extract key fields, then output clean structured JSON ready for downstream use.

Document input → Parse type & structure → Extract fields → Structured JSON output

 

# Pipeline 3: SAP Document Processing with LangChain Templates
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
import json
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
parser = StrOutputParser()
# Step 1: Parse document type and identify structure
parse_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', '''You are an SAP document analyst.
    Identify the document type and its key sections.
    Return ONLY: document_type, sections (list), primary_subject'''),
    ('human', 'Analyze this SAP document:\n{document}')
])
# Step 2: Extract fields based on document type
extract_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', '''You are an SAP document analyst.
    Document type: {doc_type}
    Extract all relevant fields for this document type.
    Return ONLY valid JSON. No explanation or preamble.'''),
    ('human', 'Extract fields from:\n{document}')
])
# Step 3: Validate and structure the extracted JSON
structure_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', '''You are a data quality validator.
    Review the extracted fields for completeness and consistency.
    Add a confidence score (0.0-1.0) and flag any missing critical fields.
    Return the final structured JSON with confidence and flags added.'''),
    ('human', 'Validate and structure:\n{extracted}')
])
# Build LangChain LCEL pipeline using pipe operator
parse_chain = parse_prompt | llm | parser
extract_chain = extract_prompt | llm | parser
structure_chain = structure_prompt | llm | parser
# Run pipeline sequentially
sap_document = '''
  Functional Spec: S4H Migration - Customer Master
  Author: jane.doe@company.com  Date: 2026-03-01
  Scope: Migrate 450,000 customer master records from ECC 6.0
  Source: KUNNR, KNA1, KNVV tables
  Target: S/4HANA BP (Business Partner) model
  Key rules: Deduplicate by tax ID, map account groups,
  preserve credit limits, validate IBAN format
  Sign-off required: Data Owner, Finance Lead
'''
 
doc_structure = parse_chain.invoke({'document': sap_document})
doc_type = 'Functional Specification'
 
extracted = extract_chain.invoke({
    'document': sap_document,
    'doc_type': doc_type
})
 
final_output = structure_chain.invoke({'extracted': extracted})
print(final_output)

LangChain's pipe operator (|) makes the chain readable and easy to modify — swap a prompt template or model at any step without touching the rest of the pipeline. The LCEL pattern also supports async execution and streaming when needed for production workloads.

 PIPELINE 4 OF 4 

  ⬡  Master Data Enrichment: Extract → Validate → Classify 

Master Data Enrichment Pipeline

Master data in SAP is rarely clean enough for direct AI consumption. This pipeline combines sequential chaining with CoT validation to enrich raw master data records: extract available attributes, apply CoT reasoning to validate completeness and consistency, then classify against a defined taxonomy. The CoT validation step is the difference between a classification pipeline and a trustworthy one.

Raw master data → Extract attributes → CoT: validate quality → Classify + confidence

 

# Pipeline 4: Master Data Enrichment with CoT Validation
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
import json
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
raw_records = [
    {'MATNR': '1000234', 'MAKTX': 'Pump assy hydraulic 250bar SAE',
     'MTART': 'ROH', 'MATKL': '', 'MEINS': 'EA', 'BRGEW': '12.5'},
    {'MATNR': '1000891', 'MAKTX': 'CHAIR OFFICE MESH BLK ERGONOMIC',
     'MTART': 'HIBE', 'MATKL': 'OFFICE', 'MEINS': 'EA', 'BRGEW': ''},
]
 
taxonomy = {
    'Mechanical Components': 'Industrial parts, assemblies, hydraulic/pneumatic',
    'Office Supplies': 'Furniture, stationery, office equipment',
    'Raw Materials': 'Unprocessed inputs for manufacturing',
    'MRO': 'Maintenance, repair and operations items',
}
 
enriched_records = []
 
for record in raw_records:
    # Step 1: Extract and normalize attributes
    step1 = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP master data expert.
        Extract and normalize attributes from this SAP material record.
        Infer missing fields where possible from the description.
        Return ONLY JSON with fields: material_number, description_clean,
        material_type, unit, weight_kg, inferred_fields (list).'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Extract from: {json.dumps(record)}')
    ]
    extracted = llm(step1).content.strip()
    # Step 2: CoT validation — reason through data quality
    step2 = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are a data quality expert.
        Reason through this master data record step by step:
        1. Are required fields present and consistent?
        2. Does the description match the material type?
        3. Are there contradictions or suspicious values?
        4. What is your overall quality confidence (High/Medium/Low)?
        Show reasoning, then conclude with: QUALITY: [level] and ISSUES: [list]'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Validate this record:\n{extracted}')
    ]
    validation = llm(step2).content.strip()
    # Step 3: Classify against taxonomy using extracted + validated data
    step3 = [
        SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify this SAP material into exactly one category.
        Taxonomy: {json.dumps(taxonomy)}
        Use the validation findings to inform confidence.
        Return ONLY JSON: category, confidence (High/Medium/Low), reason (one sentence)'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Extracted: {extracted}\nValidation: {validation}')
    ]
    classification = json.loads(llm(step3).content.strip())
 
    enriched_records.append({
        'material': record['MATNR'],
        'extracted': json.loads(extracted),
        'validation': validation,
        'classification': classification
    })
 
for r in enriched_records:
    print(f"Material {r['material']}: {r['classification']['category']} ",
          f"({r['classification']['confidence']}) — {r['classification']['reason']}")

The CoT validation step will occasionally surface data issues you did not expect — missing material groups, inconsistent units of measure, description/type mismatches. This is the pipeline working correctly. Treat validation findings as data quality signals worth feeding back to your SAP master data governance process.

 PIPELINE DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

What Makes SAP Prompt Pipelines Reliable

Across these four pipelines, a consistent set of design decisions separate reliable production output from inconsistent prototype output:

 

Design Principle

Why It Matters

One responsibility per step

Each prompt does one thing. Classify only. Summarize only. Recommend only. Combined prompts produce lower quality output across all tasks.

Pass context forward explicitly

Every downstream step receives the outputs of upstream steps as explicit context. Never assume the model remembers previous calls — it doesn't.

Use CoT for high-stakes decisions

When output needs to be auditable — risk assessments, code review, data quality — add a reasoning step before the conclusion. It costs more tokens but the output is traceable.

Enforce JSON for structured output

Instruct the model to return only JSON with no preamble when downstream code will parse the response. Claude and GPT-4o both follow this reliably when the instruction is explicit.

Choose model per step, not per pipeline

A pipeline can use different models at different steps. Use GPT-4o for HANA code steps, Claude for summarization and structured output steps. The SDK makes this a one-line change per step.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 4: Connecting to HANA Cloud Vector Store — embedding generation, REAL_VECTOR storage, RAG on your SAP data with the Generative AI Hub
  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI pipelines in your SAP extension layer
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core pipelines

If you build any of these pipelines and find that a particular step produces inconsistent output, the fix is almost always in the system prompt — either narrowing the scope of the step or making the output format instruction more explicit. Drop a comment below with the pattern and I'll share what worked in our implementation.

entry.title: Re: I have 6000 supplier records to which I want to add t...
entry.content:
For 6000 suppliers, the most practical solution is to use the Business Partner OData API to mass update tax numbers.

  • Prepare a CSV/Excel with Supplier ID + Tax Number.
  • Use middleware (SAP CPI, Postman, or a simple script) to call the API and update records.
  • This avoids the limitation of Migration Cockpit and Mass Maintenance.

entry.title: Re: How to call a custom FM once after all changed line i...
entry.content:
The item-level exits (USEREXIT_CHECK_VBAP, USEREXIT_MOVE_FIELD_TO_VBAP) are triggered once per line item, so if you put your popup logic there, you’ll naturally get multiple calls.

Below would be the Best‑practice approaches which you can explore:

1) Memory table + flag approach (common solution)
Collect all changed line items into an internal table during the item exits. Set a flag in memory (e.g., EXPORT TO MEMORY ID or a global variable in the include). Then trigger the popup once in a header‑level exit (like USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT or USEREXIT_NEW_DOCUMENT). This way, you only show the popup once, but you still have access to all changed items.

2) Use header‑level exits
Exits such as USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE or USEREXIT_NEW_DOCUMENT are called once per document, not per item. You can move the popup logic there, but you’ll need to pass the changed line items from the item exits into a global structure so they’re available.

entry.title: Re: I have 6000 supplier records to which I want to add t...
entry.content:
Dear @Paulina00_-,

In Public Cloud there is no standard Migration Cockpit object to mass-load a generic tax number into existing supplier/business partner records. SAP states that fields not delivered in the migration content are not supported by the delivered Public Cloud migration templates, even if they exist in the maintenance apps

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2683477

For a mass update of tax data for existing suppliers the supported scalable option is to use API_BUSINESS_PARTNER via communication scenario SAP_COM_0008. SAP documents that this API supports create/read/update/delete for business partner data and the Business Partner integration scenario in Public Cloud is SAP_COM_0008

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/85043858ea0f9244e10000000a4450e5.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/f7399c7374d3475a96a56a0856b5f3ee.html

So for your 6000 suppliers the recommended approach is to prepare a file with at least Business Partner number, tax number category (BPTaxType) and tax number (BPTaxNumber) then load it through the Business Partner API into entity A_BusinessPartnerTaxNumber. Before the load the required tax number categories must already be configured in the system

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/7d5f045826552246e10000000a441470.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud SO Item Ship to party Address Change
entry.content:
Since the initial ship-to party at item level is typically inherited from the original document or header level, this change is not possible.

If you need to make this change, please re-enter the (same) Ship-to Party again at item level. With this "new" ship-to party it should be possible to carry out the change.

entry.title: Re: CPI Select Datastore Operation
entry.content:
Hello! 

There is no in-order processing of data store entries. In other words, the sequence of data store entries selected with a Select operation is random and non-deterministic. If you have restricted the number of entries to be fetched (with the Number of Polled Messages attribute), the selection of retrieved entries is also random.

Find out more in the documentation.

entry.title: Re: Superpackage ZCUSTOM_DEVELOPMENT is not a valid package
entry.content:
Hi @Karthik_Gali

Yes, based on how the system is designed, the behaviour you described is expected.

In the Partner Demo Development Tenant, you are expected to create package under ZPARTNER. ZCUSTOM_DEVELOPMENT is not the valid package creation hierarchy for that landscape. If you must transport the objects in the customer 3 system track, they need to be created or copied in the correct ZCUSTOM_DEVELOPMENT package hierarchy in the appropriated custom development landscape.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Suggestions for Using Dependencies in Multi-Off Delivery
entry.content:
As a short re-cap: Multi-Off delivery is an option mainly for partners of SAP to deliver an extension in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition from the partner’s development system into the customer’s three-system landscape. The tools used for this are the Git-enabled Change and Transport System on the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition systems and a Git repository owned by the partner.

(If you don’t know Multi-Off delivery yet and would like to know more, please take a look at Multi-Off Delivery on the SAP Help Portal.)

When you clone a repository to a system, the default branch of the respective repository will be cloned into that system. But the default branch does not necessarily contain (the version of) your coding that shall become active in the target system.
When you develop with gCTS, you can make use of several branches to manage your development. You could for example use one branch for maintenance purposes, one for new features and a release branch that contains what you would like to deliver – all of that in addition to the main branch that should always be a stable one - like shown on the following image:

(If you’d like to learn more about branches, please refer to Establishing a Branching Strategy on the SAP Help Portal.)

To make sure the released coding reaches your customer and to avoid unnecessary branch switching at your customers’ side, you could – as the easiest way – make the release branch the default branch of your repository.
But this would then also mean that whenever you clone the repository newly to one of your test or development systems, this branch becomes the active branch in your (development) system. It can then easily happen that you push new coding into that branch if you forget to switch the branch after cloning. You can take measures on the Git side to make sure that branches are protected. You should especially protect the release branch against direct commits. Please check in the documentation of your respective Git provider how this is done. Protected branches then require special handling. The merge of a feature branch into a protected release branch can be initiated in the gCTS app, but the merge result cannot be pushed any more to the remote repository. To solve this, you would have to create a new branch (split off from the old or default branch) and fill it with the merge result via a pull request.

So how do we get around this complicated way of working?

There are different options:

  • One option is to create a branch that always remains empty. Make this branch the default branch of your repository. You should then also always protect this branch against writing operations. You can use the main branch for this purpose. After the repository has been cloned, your customers would need to switch to the desired branch to get your development into their systems.
  • Another option is to use dependencies. In gCTS you can define dependencies between repositories. One of the possible dependency definitions is to define a dependency on a certain branch of another repository. And that is what would help solving the issue. Let's look into this option in more detail.

You create two repositories. Let’s call them ‘delivery’ and ‘development’.

The delivery repo consists of just one main branch with no ABAP coding – just a commit with the properties file. In the properties of this delivery repo, you define a dependency on a release branch of the development repo.

In there, click Create.

Define the data of your development repository and make sure that you select the release branch.

After you clicked Create, the dependency is created:

In your development system, the dependency is shown as red as the dependency is not correctly implemented. The release branch of the development is most of the time not the active branch in that system. But that doesn’t matter. You don’t rely on the dependency in your development system.

You would then tell your customers to clone the delivery repo. When they do so, automatically, the dependency manager would make sure that the release branch of the development repo would be cloned, as well.

 

Cloning dependencies is always done via a job. You can select to do it immediately or at a later point in time.

 

When the job is done, both repositories are cloned and the release branch of the development repo is active.

 

All other branches of the development repository are not available locally on the customers' systems.

 

When you later provide updates in the development repository, your customers can update to the latest commit in that repository. They don’t need to do anything in or with the delivery repository.

If you work with tags, you should only create tags in the release branch of the development repository.

entry.title: How to Use a gCTS Repository to Share Your Development Across System Landscape
entry.content:
A Git repository is made for working together on a development project. You can collaborate or share your work with others. gCTS in SAP S/4HANA allows you to do so, as well. In most cases, you might use gCTS within one system landscape to have a place where your ABAP coding can be pushed to and where it is available to be pulled into other systems inside your company.

But why not use this option to share your development with other companies or with your customers if you are a consultant or partner? Also if you work in a company that is organized in several subsidiaries and where the system landscapes of the different branches are not connected or managed in one datacenter, it might be beneficial to take Git into consideration as a sharing option for your custom coding.

If you take some points into consideration, you can easily do so.

First and foremost, for what is described in this blog post, it is required that both parties use systems that are either SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition or that run on-premise in the respective companies.

(If you would like to share coding in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, please consider the Multi-Off Delivery.)

Also, gCTS increases its functionality from release to release. If the release of your source system is higher than the one of possible target systems, it can happen that some e.g. configurations that you did for your repository don’t work. One example is the dependency management. With this, you can make sure that dependent repositories are updated or cloned when the repository is updated. But this functionality has been introduced newly with SAP S/4HANA 2025. So, if you make use of it in your source system, but the target party still uses an older release, the dependencies cannot be pulled.

Main prerequisite is for sure that every team or company that needs your coding accepts to work with gCTS. This includes that they activate gCTS in their systems and then pull coding from a Git repository.

If that is clarified, the one who provides the coding needs to allow access to the consumers of to their repository. This can be done by creating a token that the consumers can then add to the credentials store in their gCTS app. And that’s mainly it: if the consumers have connected their systems to the repository, they can then pull coding from the repository.

Done! 😊

But there are some aspects that you should take into consideration:
First, you cannot be sure that the consumers have the same release of an ABAP system in place as the one that you used for development. If their release is higher, this shouldn’t be a problem. But what if the release on the consumer side is lower? There is no guarantee that the coding is working.

To make sure that the two systems fit together, you should maintain prerequisites for your branch or repository. This is done on the Properties tab in the gCTS app. You can define a Component and its Release. If you need it, also an SP can be defined. But please note that you can only define exactly one release, there is no option that allows e.g. something like ‘higher than’. So, if you want to offer your development for several releases, you could create a separate branch for each release – make sure that each branch contains the complete development and not only parts of it.

 This prerequisite is going to be stored in the properties file of the repository. If the prerequisite is not met on a system, the repository cannot be cloned. Please read the Set Prerequisites of a Repository section in the SAP Help Portal to learn more. Please make sure that the respective branch contains the coding that is expected to work with the release that you defined as a prerequisite.

In addition, you might want to make sure that the consumer pulls a defined state of your development, not an intermediate one that you might currently have available in the default branch of your repository (as gCTS always clones the default branch when you clone a new repository to your system). For this it might be a good plan to leave the default branch empty and create separate branches per release. The consumer would then at first clone the empty branch of that repository (which means that no coding of yours arrives in the system) and only after that, they would switch to the branch that fits their system or needs. But this might also have effects on your company-internal setup. Instead, you can use a little trick and create a repository that has a dependency to the required branch of your development repository. If you are interested in doing so, please read the blog post Suggestion for using dependencies in Multi-Off Delivery. The same is possible in repositories in SAP Cloud ERP Private as well.

entry.title: Downtime Optimized Conversion Evolution - “Uptime Customizing for FI conversion”
entry.content:
From Pilot to Global Standard: Uptime Customizing for FI Conversion in Downtime-Optimized S/4HANA Conversions

Large scale SAP S/4HANA conversions and migrations often face a fundamental constraint: business cannot tolerate long downtime. For global systems processing billions of transactions, extended downtime is simply not an option.

To address this challenge, SAP introduced the Downtime Optimized Conversion (DoC) approach within SUM (Software Update Manager). It moves time consuming database migration and data model conversions to SUM uptime phases with only "delta" processing in downtime

Over time, this approach has now evolved further with the introduction of Uptime Customizing for FI Conversion.

This article shares some of the architecture decisions, achievements, challenges, and lessons learned during a project where we leveraged this technology. The project served as a pilot implementation for "Uptime Customizing for FI Conversion", and its success contributed to the feature becoming generally available (GA) in later SUM releases post successful go live.

The Downtime Challenge in Large SAP Transformations

Enterprise SAP landscapes often support mission-critical operations such as:

  • Global financial activities and consolidation

  • Payroll for hundreds of thousands of employees

  • Transaction processing across multiple business units and regions

For such environments, even short outages can impact business operations/monetary losses and regulatory obligations.

Standard conversion using SUM require large portions of system migration and data model conversion activities to occur during the technical downtime window. Downtime Optimized Conversion addresses this by moving large parts of the migration workload into the system uptime phase, significantly reducing the duration of technical downtime.

Downtime Optimized Conversion - A refresher

 

The traditional DoC approach shifts selected activities of a standard conversion into uptime processing, including:

  • Data migration

  • Field conversions/FIN and ML data model conversions.

  • Migration of selected large tables

The below diagram would give a refresher:

 

However, earlier DoC still had some major drawbacks: Majorly a customizing freeze required in the production system. This was because:

  1. FIN customizing (on S/4HANA level) is set up during standard conversion run, "stored" in a transport request. The transport request is included using CTI (Customer transport integration) buffer used for subsequent DoC cycles. Since DoC does FI conversion in uptime it needs the FI customizing adjustments for S/4HANA to do the data model conversions.
  2. A new standard run is required whenever FIN customizing (on actual production system) changes as previously prepared CTI has no adjustments for the changes. No possibility of adapting changes directly in a new DoC cycle.
  3. Customizing soft freeze with a lot of restrictions in the actual production system is required to maintain the viability of "Stored" transport request.

This created several project challenges:

  • Customizing changes had to stop for extended periods
  • Multiple standard conversion cycles were required
  • Any new business configuration changes in production forced additional cycles

For organizations with high number of configuration changes due to the nature of their business, this constraint significantly slowed project progress, resulting in increased costs and efforts with extra cycles, increasing the overall project timeline and reducing flexibility of changes allowed in the production system for extended periods. 

For simplicity, I will refer to the previous DoC as "DOC - legacy" and new DoC as "DOC - UCFC" in this article.

Introducing Uptime Customizing for FI Conversion

 

To address these limitations, a new enhancement was introduced: Uptime Customizing for FI Conversion (UCFC)

With this capability, FI "delta" customizing can be executed during SUM uptime in the temporary system instance itself.

Remember that an initial standard cycle at the start of the project with a copy of production system is still required to capture "Initial FI customizing for S/4HANA" transport. Following that any customizing changes in production can be adjusted in temporary instance in a new cycle. The users can dynamically perform missing customizing steps for new configuration (i.e. new plants, company codes etc.) in a new cycle during the uptime phase itself.

This approach can drastically reduce project timelines. Below diagram illustrates it:

 Initial complete FI Customizing                           Delta FI customizing

With every cycle, you can do "Delta customizing" on the Temp instance and include those "Delta customizing" TRs in the CTI buffer.

 

Achievements possible due to DoC - Uptime customizing for FI conversion:

 

  • Saved resources, efforts, cost and time (>4-5 Months) of at least four “Standard” conversion cycles which would have needed to be run with the old DoC Technology/Approach but were not needed with DoC-UCFC.
  • Eliminated a need of change freeze of more than >19-20 weeks in production system.
  • Migrated and converted an ECC instance with >40TB database size, including billions of ACDOCA records, and billions of generated DoC delta CRR records with zero critical data loss.
  • Cut downtime from 95+ hours (Standard conversion) to just 15 hours — an 85% improvement — while integrating >2,300 customer transports in the same SUM technical downtime (these alone would have added ~10 hours).

With this project go-live, DoC - “Uptime Customizing for Finance Conversion” was made GA (Generally available) with SUM 2.0 SP23 and is the new default Downtime-Optimized Conversion technology since May 2025.

 

Technology changes enabling UCFC:

 

The key difference in the evolved technology that enables "delta customizing" to be possible in the SUM created Temp instance (TMP) is migration of ALL application tables in uptime. This is explained in detail below:

An example of tables processed during DoC - UCFC compared to DoC - Legacy:

A much higher count of tables being handled in uptime processing of SUM means some new challenges to manage as part of the project.

 

Challenges/Potential pitfalls to manage:

 

More “Read-Only” Tables

Many more tables get “Read-only” flag as it now depends on the “Delivery Class” of a table. More chances of having an unplanned business restriction especially if a table’s “Delivery Class” is strictly not adhered to by Application. i.e. Tables categorized as "Customizing" but having mixed-use as both Transactional/Application tables and master data tables may get a read-only flag during SUM uptime and result in a business process being restricted during SUM uptime. Detailed Impact analysis via SUM toolbox becomes more crucial than ever.

Example: Some processes like "Run regeneration programs for Substitution/validation rules" may access customizing tables and fail in case the underlying tables are set to "read-only". Usually, the processes like these can be identified and run early before even starting SUM uptime to avoid impact.

  • Impact analysis for “Read-only” table is now much more crucial. Gather production use statistics for each week for several months. Track every conflict (Read-only table against number of changes in production) and map to exact business process if possible.
  • Convince customer to do some business testing in earlier cycles during SUM uptime to catch as many conflicts as early as possible.
  • Be prepared for surprises in “Load Verification cycle”. Following LV, decisions can be taken regarding these tables for actual go live.
  • Very Important: Plan SUM uptime during go-live in the same weeks of month as done for LV cycles. Example, If LV cycle ran for first 2 weeks of the month and Go-live cycle runs in the last two weeks of a month, then go-live may have the customer running new business processes like Month-end close that may have new conflicts never found in LV cycle!

"Load verification Cycle" -- A cycle where SUM "uptime" only is done entirely on production in advance to check all impacts. SUM is reset from downtime confirmation screen in actual production system while the downtime is executed on a clone. Running LV cycles during a downtime minimization project leveraging technologies like Downtime optimized conversions or ZDO (Zero downtime option) is crucial to a project's success.

 

More uptime tables with Change recording and replay (CRR) via triggers

Since we have much more tables with "Change recording and Replay" in DoC-UCFC as compared to DoC-Legacy, it means more “Delta Recording” changes to be replayed before downtime.

During early validation cycles on production, several billion transactional changes were captured through change-recording triggers. Despite several days of replication, only about 94% of the recorded changes could be replayed before the downtime window began. The remaining delta had to be replicated during downtime, which added additional hours to the technical downtime.

Further analysis revealed an important pattern. Many tables with very small data size but extremely high change rates were contributing disproportionately to the total volume of recorded changes. Although these tables were small in size, they generated hundreds of millions of changes, making them inefficient candidates for uptime migration.

Based on this observation, the migration strategy was refined by excluding certain high-change, low-sized tables from uptime migration and instead migrating them during downtime. The low size meant it added just seconds/minutes to downtime migration. This was achieved using CRRTABLIST.LST file as documented in SAP Note 3444013. 

This optimization delivered two key improvements:

  • The uptime window was shortened and better controlled.

  • The total volume of delta changes was reduced significantly.

As a result, during the production go-live cycle the system achieved 100% replication of recorded changes before the downtime phase, allowing the downtime window to proceed without additional replication delays. 

So previous DoC, you had to think of “Including big tables” in uptime, now you should think of “Excluding tables” from uptime for customer systems with “Very High” transactional volume/change rate but not that high size, especially for customers with “Very busy” systems.

However, be careful of what you move to downtime as it should not be a table which may be needed for FI conversion in uptime. Whenever in doubt submit an incident to BC-UPG-TLS-TLA or BC-UPG-DTM_TLA. Our product support experts can guide you.

 

CTI (Customer transport integration) Buffer and Retrofits

With Uptime Customizing for FI conversion, FI configuration is handled through the CTI buffer, which contains the transports generated during earlier conversion cycles. While this enables the system to execute FI conversion during uptime, it also introduces a new challenge: the CTI buffer may contain older customizing transports created months earlier in the project. Especially, the initial customizing FI transports from standard cycle which may be up to a year old or more depending on the length of your project.

If production customizing changes occur later in the project, these older transports in the CTI buffer can unintentionally overwrite newer production configuration during the conversion. This risk becomes more significant in long-running transformation programs where the initial FI customizing snapshot may be several months old.

To address this, a strong retrofit strategy is required between the source system and the S/4HANA landscape. Ideally, all relevant retrofits—including simple changes that require no functional adjustment—should be incorporated into the CTI buffer for each conversion cycle so that the customizing state remains aligned with the current production system.

In practice, managing this balance requires careful governance. Including too many transports in the CTI buffer can introduce new classification changes or read-only table situations (Especially with a lot of VDAT/CDAT objects), while excluding them may require post-conversion corrections.

During the project, we adopted a controlled CTI strategy, where the buffer was stabilized at certain points in the project timeline and only essential delta customizing changes were added afterward. Other transports were handled post-conversion when necessary. This approach helped maintain conversion stability while still allowing ongoing project development activities in parallel (N+1) Landscape.

We were able to include around 2300+ customer transports in the CTI buffer in SUM which would have taken another 10+ hours of import had they not been included in SUM.

 

Delta Replication Challenges – Database Log Management Limitations

During the project, a major operational challenge was observed related to the database log management behavior of older DB2 versions.

In this environment, both committed and uncommitted transactions share the same active log space, and advanced log space management available in later database versions was not present. This created a risk during DoC delta replication, where a large number of migration processes (R3loads) continuously generate commits.

Under normal conditions this replication is stable, but if even a single long-running uncommitted transaction is active, it can hold the oldest log entry and prevent log reuse. As replication commits continue to accumulate, the active log can rapidly fill up, causing num_log_span violations potentially causing transaction rollbacks or even system instability.

During early validation cycles, log usage increased sharply shortly after SUM delta replication using CRR (Change, record and replay framework) started, highlighting the need for careful control of replication activity.

To mitigate this risk, the execution strategy included:

  • Identifying long-running jobs that could hold open transactions (Like BW extraction jobs).

  • Carefully limiting the number of replication processes running concurrently.

  • Implementing continuous log monitoring during uptime replication and pausing/reducing replication processes (R3loads) when log usage spikes were detected.

These controls ensured stable delta replication throughout the migration cycles.

 

Performance Issues

Another important issue identified during the project involved database lock contention caused by high update parallelism and DoC inline triggers. More tables in uptime means more triggers and more triggers may cause unanticipated performance issues and need to be identified by running load verification/load simulation cycles.

Financial postings triggered updates across multiple related ledger tables as part of a single logical unit of work. When many update processes attempted to modify the same row simultaneously, the system experienced database lock waits, causing update processes to queue behind each other.

Although lock waits appeared on the first table in the update sequence, the underlying cause was the cumulative update latency across several tables in the transaction flow. The presence of DoC inline triggers slightly increased update processing time, which amplified lock contention under very high parallelism.

Because update requests were distributed across many application servers, a large number of update work processes competed to update the same data rows. This resulted in:

  • rapidly increasing database lock waits

  • a growing backlog of pending update records

  • other business activities temporarily waiting for update processes to become available

 As we reduced the update work processes instead of increasing them, the updates flowed faster and contention was released. Understanding this behavior helped refine the system configuration and operational monitoring during the migration window to ensure stable execution of business transactions during uptime processing.

 

Additional Resources

Simplify the downtime-optimized System Conversion ... - SAP Community

Support Portal Page of Software Update Manager: https://support.sap.com/en/tools/software-logistics-tools/software-update-manager.html

Support Portal Page of downtime-optimized conversion approach of Software Update Manager: https://support.sap.com/en/tools/software-logistics-tools/software-update-manager/downtime-optimized...

SAP Note on Uptime Customizing for FI Conversion: SAP Note 3444013

Feel free to comment below.

The BADI used is `EDOC_CO_ADAPT_XML_INVOICE`.

entry.title: Why Solid Data Foundations Matter More Than Flashy AI
entry.content:
From all the conversations I have been having the past years since the release of ChatGPT, AI is totally underhyped and overhyped at the same time. From doomsday speculations to AI being useless, I have heard it all. We all have heard it all. But what I am missing in all these discussions is differentiation. It is the simultaneity of these things.

While AI might possibly turn on us one day, we should already now be looking at the risks and some of which have been there way before the release of ChatGPT. AI algorithms steering public opinion through social media, AI already in the hands of people with questionable morals and in the case of business AI we need to watch out for hallucinations, AI systems with unreliable data foundations, and biases in AI that can cause harm especially when human data is involved.

While AI might not solve all our problems today, it is far from useless. We just need to understand how it works, adjust our expectations accordingly and give it the necessary tools and information to get the results we need.

Unlocking AI’s Potential Starts with Rethinking Your Data

One of the biggest problems that companies have today is finding good use cases that lead to business value and getting their company ready for the AI age. We can build the best AI agents and retrieval augmented generation (RAG) workflows, if we do not have our data available with the logic, key metrics and relationships intact, then it is extremely difficult for AI agents to make good use of it.

We also must start thinking about our data differently. AI agents might need data differently than the data we already store in our systems. We might need to think about additional fields in our datasets to help AI make decisions the way we do. We need to capture more sentiment, give more explanations as to why we make certain decisions.

We expect magic from systems built on disorganized data, yet we’re often unwilling to invest the time and resources needed to structure and clean that data properly. This disconnect leads to disappointment when AI fails to deliver, not because the technology lacks potential, but because its foundation is shaky.

Imagine how powerful an AI agent could be when it has access to your relational data, understand your business data and has access to powerful tools such as SAP-RPT-1, data that is spread over documents and internal wikis, models such as perplexity or custom machine learning models. Such an agent could seamlessly analyze complex datasets, draw actionable insights, automate decision-making processes, and deliver tailored recommendations in real time, all while continuously learning from new information across your organization.

Do we need AI to be boring again?

The truth is: many AI agents being built today are reaching too high. While everyone loves flashy AI use cases right now and stages are full of autonomously moving browser windows, voice calls to AI agents and robots sorting laundry, the real power of business AI lies in the processes that work quietly behind the scenes.

I still hear of countless processes where people map data from A to B and manually copy cells between Excel sheets. These are the quiet inefficiencies that drain thousands of hours where AI can create immediate, meaningful impact.

But none of this works without the right data foundation. Newer models might hallucinate less, but “less” is often not enough for businesses. What we need is often not more data but the right data. Context‑rich, structured, connected, and reflective of how decisions are actually made within the company.

Another mistake I often see and why I believe AI needs to be a little more boring again is that, everyone wants to build AI agents now but we should stop firing cannons at sparrows. Not every problem needs an AI agent. Some require classical ML or simple RAG workflows and some require no AI at all.

To achieve this companies must upskill alongside their employees, not only technically but also ethically. As developers working with AI today we get to shape the future and how AI will be used going forward. Let’s make sure, we do that wisely.

To summarize: agents only work when they have reliable, consolidated information. AI use cases are only as successful as the data fabric that feeds them. This is exactly why we at SAP believe in the power of SAP Business Data Cloud.

 

So what does it take to build a data fabric that is robust, reliable, and rich with business context?

For developers tasked with shaping the future of AI, this is the critical question. To find answers, join us for the Fabric of Data & AI event. You’ll learn directly from industry leaders how a business data fabric connects different data sources, metadata, and processes to keep AI grounded in real business context. This is your opportunity to learn how to build the solid foundations that turn AI’s potential into actual business outcomes.

The Fabric of Data & AI virtual event on March 24: https://url.sap/vlm80u

entry.title: Re: How can employees be made ineligible for Variable Pay...
entry.content: In the absence of using a custom MDF, you could load your list into a static RBP group (static allows you to easily upload a list) and then in your business rule use the function that says "Is User in RBP Group" and reference the group and select "Yes" (if the list are those who are eligible). Then anyone in that group would get their history pulled. Or you could do the reverse and put ineligible's in the list and then say "Is User in RBP group" and say "No" and it would pull those not listed. I've not used this method for variable pay history but I assume it would work. I am using this method for regular form eligibility for a client to manage exceptions to their broader eligibility rules.

The best practice is to have consistent entitlements across productive and non-productive instances to avoid developing things in one tier that cannot be tested/ verified as working properly before or in Production. If you want to deploy an AI based scenario in production, you have to first develop it in a Dev landscape, which will not be possible in a mixed landscape. 

The purchase of additional messages is also an option for enhanced edition, just like other editions of SAP Integration Suite under the same pricing structure.

entry.title: Re: Is there a Migration Object related to Project Hours...
entry.content:
Dear @hajersoula,

In Public Cloud there is currently no delivered Migration Cockpit object for importing project-related timesheet entries (hours per person per day posted to Project/WBS). If such entries need to be loaded in mass so the supported standard option is integration via the Manage Workforce Timesheet API (communication scenario SAP_COM_0027) not a standard migration object. SAP also documents this API for mass processing scenarios

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2707996

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b08cb360201544ec803330fefa59b0f7/fa198e62c6d24788bd1f82082feb3096.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/20607b6de19a4d34a41b0abec6186b01.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Old Tile Still Appearing in SAP Build Work Zone After Complete Re‑Exposure from S/4HANA
entry.content:
@OmPrakashJha 

First, ensure that in the backend (tr-cd: /ui2/cdm3_exp_scope) that the roles are successfully exposed. You can check the exposed contents there.

Next, check the report in the content provider after refreshing. Does the updated role count match the number of roles actually updated? There are cases where refreshing looks successful but the count of updated content are zero.

If you are using content exposure version V2 in /ui2/cdm3_exp_scope, make sure to map the alias FLP_STANDALONE to the content provider. 

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-standard-edition/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition-on-china-shanghai-region/federation-of-remote-content-providers

entry.title: Missing plant from Starter System
entry.content:
We have just started our SAP Cloud ERP project and have started "Define Scope" step in the CBC. We have chosen and activated the scope item 1P7 - Cross plant transfer posting. In the Test Script, the transfer posting requires Plant DIGITS and plant DIGITS. 

However, when we go to the Organisational Structure step only plant DIGITS is available. Plant DIGITS is not there. Please advise how we can get Plant DIGITS in the Organisational Structure for the CBC for Starter System


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Missing plant from Starter System
entry.content:
Hi!

For scope item 1P7 – Cross-Plant Transfer Posting, the test script demonstrates the process using two plants (for example Plant 6910 and Plant 6920 when New Zealand is selected in your Evaluation Workspace) in order to illustrate a cross-plant scenario.

However, in the Organizational Structure step of SAP Central Business Configuration, it is expected that only the plant initially created in the workspace may be available (for example Plant 6910). If an additional plant is required to execute the scenario, it must be created manually in the organizational structure.

The Test Script documentation clarifies this point:

Flex.Org App or Set Up Organizational (SAP Central Business Configuration) extension

Note: The second set of organization entity IDs here is an example. In an evaluation project of the SAP Central Business Configuration environment, the ID follows the naming rule described in Using the Set Up Organizational Structure App.

This means that the second plant shown in the test script is only an example used to illustrate the cross-plant process, and it is not automatically created in the system.

If you are working in an Evaluation workspace connected to a Starter System, you can create an additional plant using the Set Up Organizational Structure app in SAP Central Business Configuration so that the scenario can be executed in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

When creating organizational units in Evaluation workspaces, please follow these naming guidelines:

  • Distribution channels and divisions: create alphanumeric IDs with at least one alphabetic character.

  • Other organizational units (such as plants): create alphanumeric IDs where the last two characters are alphabetic.

Please refer to KBA: 3727800 - Cross-Plant Transfer Posting requires second Plant not available in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me

Therefore, if your scenario requires two plants, simply create an additional plant in the organizational structure following the naming guidelines for evaluation workspaces.

For additional details about the naming requirement or Organizational Structure, you may also refer to

  • 3487303 - Unable to create organizational unit due to naming requirement in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me
  • Using the Set Up Organizational Structure App | SAP Help Portal
  • Setting Up Organizational Structure | SAP Help Portal

Best Regards!

entry.title: Is SAP ABAP developer still a good career option in 2026 for someone starting fresh in IT?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
I’m a 22-year-old Computer Science graduate currently navigating the transition from academia to the professional world. Over the past few months of job hunting, I’ve done some deep soul-searching regarding my technical strengths.
While I have a solid grasp of theory, system design, and "under-the-hood" fundamentals, I’ve realized that high-pressure, DSA-heavy competitive coding rounds aren't where I shine. I am a logical thinker who enjoys building structured, business-oriented solutions rather than solving abstract algorithmic puzzles.
This has led me to research the SAP Ecosystem, specifically ABAP, as a career path. I’m particularly drawn to the structured nature of enterprise software. However, I have a few questions for the experts here:
  1. Market Viability in 2026: For someone starting fresh today, is the demand for ABAP developers still strong, specifically with the industry-wide shift to S/4HANA?
  2. The AI Impact: How is SAP Joule and Generative AI changing the daily workflow of an ABAPer? Does a "Clean Core" strategy mean more or less work for custom developers?
  3. The "Full-Stack" SAP Dev: I see a lot of talk about SAP BTP, Fiori, and RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming). Should a fresher aim to be a "Pure ABAPer," or is it essential to start with a BTP-first mindset?
  4. Career Longevity: Coming from a non-competitive coding background, can I build a high-growth career here? Does the SAP world value system-design thinking over raw coding speed?
I’m eager to contribute to the enterprise space and want to ensure I’m investing my time in a future-proof stack,  I’d love to hear from those of you currently in the trenches.
Thank you for your time and guidance!
